item.title: NEW IMAGE
item.content:encoded:
A suitable new image has been located for this skip under CC-BY-SA-2.0

The original is by "Unhindered by Talent", and can be found here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So there are a few too many holes in this one for my tastes. When you start dealing with something as fiddly as temporal regression of technology (never mind sociopolitical entities and ideas) it is very easy for the wheels to come off. That's what seems to have happened here in my opinion. The post about the gas lamps was a great example of this. Depending on the type of gas lamps involved and if the tech is converted whole cloth or piece by piece they would be a terrible choice. If they revert whole cloth then you would have a smoke filled, searing hot, very dark room. If it is piece by piece then the valves and piping would become useless right away. Just so I'm clear here the lamps are just one of many ways I can see this scip not working. -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I usually dislike skips set in the future, they just don't jibe with my head canon.
If it's really the year 3058, why are all these ancient 21st century archives posted on the hypernet like they're relevant?

But this not only does the gimmick elegantly and organic to the anomaly; it may have provided the framework for me to accept future skips in general.

How am I reading article X from the next generation while sitting here in 2019? Because that particular file server is beyond the current rollback radius. Head canon resolved!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

As this is an article that deals with temporal effects, it would make sense to crosslink it to the frequently referenced Temporal Anomalies Department.

Editing this:

Special Containment Procedures: Item SCP-1297 is contained in the center of a 10 x 10 x 5 m reinforced concrete enclosure, with one bulk-head door accessible via the east wall at floor level.

To this:

Special Containment Procedures: By order of the Temporal Anomalies Department, Item SCP-1297 is contained in the center of a 10 x 10 x 5 m reinforced concrete enclosure, with one bulk-head door accessible via the east wall at floor level.

Additionally, SCP-1297-1 instances were later referenced in another SCP by the same author, and another crosslink could be added there. Editing this:

In addition, SCP-1297-1 is capable of spontaneous animation. Within five minutes of emergence, SCP-1297-1 will solidify around any ejected clipping in a radially symmetric pattern similar to an invertebrate of class Asteroidea. Resulting animate instances are no greater than 0.25 m in diameter, but are capable of two (2) meter vertical jumps, leaps at a length of five (5) meters, and movement in any direction at speeds of up to 20 km/h, which is employed to put as much distance between SCP-1297 and SCP-1297-1 as possible. Instances appear to be aware of their surroundings, and have been known to remove additional clippings from SCP-1297 before attempting escape.

To this:

In addition, SCP-1297-1 is capable of spontaneous animation. Within five minutes of emergence, SCP-1297-1 will solidify around any ejected clipping in a radially symmetric pattern similar to an invertebrate of class Asteroidea. Resulting animate instances are no greater than 0.25 m in diameter, but are capable of two (2) meter vertical jumps, leaps at a length of five (5) meters, and movement in any direction at speeds of up to 20 km/h, which is employed to put as much distance between SCP-1297 and SCP-1297-1 as possible. Instances appear to be aware of their surroundings, and have been known to remove additional clippings from SCP-1297 before attempting escape. Entities similar to SCP-1297-1 have been observed in relation to the SCP-3109 anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for fridge horror: if "the present day" only exists because there's a toenail running around loose and the time distortion has encompassed the whole planet, what happens if the Foundation ever finds that toenail and gets it back in the jar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a painting!?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah, goddamnit I don't get it. I mean it's good, but that bit at the end is tying my brain up in knots. I think I might PM the creator of this one just to find out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If only it could be sent into space…
I am proud to be the owner of your hundredth upvote. Congrats, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Decomposing toenails alone give me the creeps. But the effects and the letter story also did it for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"NO, no get out of my house! Who are you?"
"Sir, we know what you're hiding."
"I'm not hiding anything, I swear, just let my family go!"
"We know it's here somewhere."
"What? What do you want from me?"
"You know perfectly well what we want."
"No, I don't. Just tell me, and I'll give it to you if you have it."
"We want the toenail."
"A…a toenail? You're doing all this for a toenail? Do you want me to cut them, I have some clippers I can-"
"NO! It's a very specific toenail, from the year 2049. DNA evidence suggests it belongs to you."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I feel like there's something I'm missing regarding the 2049 thing, other than the delta-t.

And second, this feels like the kind of article where the words "Challenge Accepted" were uttered.

Still +1 for painting a jar of toenails as menacing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went from not liking the randomness of the effect at the first half to really liking the letter and the example of how it could effect life as we know it at the second half. Awesome job with this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is rather similar to events in Philip K. Dick's Ubik - it features a world that degrades in time, like in the scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had hoped you would choose this one to feature, I'm glad you did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has it occurred to you the 2049 thing will be lost on readers in 35 years, if the site's still around?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a bit conflicted. This is a cool item, it's physically very weird and its properties are interesting. The first half of the article hooks me pretty good.

On the other hand, the bit about how it can break containment at will feels tacked on just to make the item scarier and to make sure we can call it "keter toenails". I'm not sure it really adds anything to the story and I'm not sure the letter at the end does either.

Still probably gonna upvote though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This effect reminds me a lot of similar effects in the old, but excellent, tabletop RPG Torg. In that, it was a consequence of colliding realities. The regression of technological objects doesn't sit well with me, because it seems to presuppose some VERY important things about the nature of reality: namely that it is virtual, Platonic, or something similar. I don't mind that presupposition, if it were at least touched upon.
The conceptual regression (which I believe was noted) seems more problematic (and I can't take credit for these implications - Torg handled it extensively). As socio-political concepts regressed, certain organizational systems would regress or vanish. What happens when the concept of a Foundation becomes foreign, or the containment philosophy? I could imagine that even if the agent-turned-"Beowulf" reached the center, destruction might trump re-containment. Having 50 guards might be a liability if stone-age tribal structure shrinks small enough that 50 "hunters" cannot cooperate. Also, not only would weapons regress, but weapons training would likewise regress. This could be useful (SMG marksmanship regressing to talent with crossbows, for example), but would mean that if things regressed back far enough, there might be no structured fighting "styles" left to use.

Those muddy concepts are enough for me to downvote, but I liked the bit at the end enough to just remain neutral. Very clever, and if I hadn't already been overexposed to a similar idea, I might have upvoted.

Edit: I just noticed that situational knowledge remains intact. That would help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
End, it has begun, now I am free,
Your ending sails with me
My serpent son stirs up the sea;
The Ship of Nails breaks free.

item.title: Image Source
item.content:encoded:
Source for the image of this article is this blog post by blogger "'Dam Divine". They are apparently his finger and toenail clippings, collected over 20 years, which were discarded.

Although the page does not list any copyright information, I'm going to try and contact this blogger to ask for specific permission. Meantime, if he sees his toenails and would like them to be removed, I will do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2049?
Oh crap it got us already didn't it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderfully inexplicable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, I was a little turned off by what I felt was an overdone "No, really, we're having a hard time containing it because it keeps finding ways to get out" property that seems to frequently pop up for things with malign effects where the author wants to forestall "So, if bad things happen when we do Thing A, why not stop doing thing A", but when the addendum clarified that this was a form of protest against the Foundation and its ability to escape containment was likely engineered in, that made things much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you go back enough centuries…

You're right, but if you'll notice, technology regresses prior to its disappearance. My thought was that natural gas is the top of an 'open flame lighting' tech tree, and that they would regress to torches.

Then shouldn't the guards all be wearing something that covers all their skin…?

My thought was that standard tactical gear for security guards includes gloves (due to the fuck-ton retarded amount of SCiPs that are activated by skin contact), but I suppose that might need to be explicitly stated. I'll consider it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just the fact that this SCP is about Keter toenails gets my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The room is lit with natural gas torches,

If you go back enough centuries, that technology wouldn't be around either, so shouldn't real torches be used? And shouldn't they have oil lanterns with a flint-and-steel available?

Direct skin contact with the humor causes intense abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting in 100% of subjects. Olfaction was sufficient to induce effects in 13% of those exposed.

Then shouldn't the guards all be wearing something that covers all their skin, plus have available a real primitive version of a gas mask (like a cloth face mask soaked in water)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the… nice twist at the end. Enjoy your +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this one in chat and I love this one even more now. Take your +1 with pride.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I likewise downvoted this. Liked the letter and its implications, but the rest wasn't to my taste.

Now VAE, your post got me thinking: What's the most downvotes on an article that's positively rated? Probably on 682 or 579.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck yeah, Keter toenails.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it was changed after the post.

Anyways, sure it is, and at the end of the day, my downvote is just one - it's when you have dozens of people clicking the little minus that you're in trouble x3.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naglfar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I think that the statement about proficiency was ok. I mean, it's not like you can tell *precisely* to which point the regression will progress. And anyone proficient with a simple blunt melee weapon (ex. a club, made from wood, bone or whatever hard material) will be more than enough. Even chimps can use such tools, while making a rapier or a sword requires more technical knowledge. And good steel or iron have been around just for several thousands of years (before that, bronze).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanted to let you know, I did think a lot about your objections, and addressed a few of them. For instance the section you quoted above does not appear in the current version of the document, and has been re-written for clarity.

I definitely value your input, but in the end this is still the story I wanted to tell. No hard feelings, of course. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've addressed a few of your concerns. thanks for the tips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I explained my reasons for disliking this in PM when I was looking over the draft, and since nothing has changed, I'll just paste it here.

Hmm
First of all, why is this Keter? I mean, sure, it somehow broke out of a steel block, but given there's no detail given, it just feels like something for it to do that'd make it Keter. I mean… even with technology it takes a little time to start fucking up . . it almost feels like you could set it up in a way that propelled chunks would just fall back in - you know, fit a sheet metal cone around the entrance , like those injured cat / dog collars.

Agents with experience using antique and ancient weaponry are to be preferred.

I dislike this as well. I mean, why don't you specify what they have to be proficient with, and if you need it . .. the Foundation can train the people it needs.

From outside the active radius, changes can be observed in clothing, speech, and behavior of persons inside the anomaly. Additionally, technology, building materials and machines will first regress to simpler forms before disappearing completely once temporal displacement becomes sufficient to preclude the object's existence. SCP-XXXX will also change appearance as the event progresses2.

Okay, well. I can't say I'm a huge fan of the effect, and it sort of comes out from the blue.
Also, what with personnel reverting. I mean, if they revert to a time before this was contained, they wouldn't know the containment procedures, would they?

And then you have the shit-covered clippings that exploit the fact. . wait.
What about waxed cloth? That sort of "hazmat" protection would likely prevent contact with the shit, yet was available for ages!

I dunno. This one kinda does little for me overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not really bad, but what earned this my downvote are the following issues:

At least 50 guards are to remain on site at all times in event of attempted breach, with no fewer than five (5) Security personnel stationed on the catwalk at all times.

Security shouldn't be capitalized, fifty (50) guards should be using enumeration formatting, and honestly 50 is both rather suspiciously round and strangely high. Why precisely 50?

Agents demonstrating proficiency with antique and ancient weaponry are to be preferred.

Firstly, an agent and an armed guard aren't necessarily the same thing. We draw a lot of parallels from Men in Black, but we're much more reasonable. Secondly, this is silly. "Guard personnel must receive additional training in the use of antique firearms and fencing." would make much more sense.

[…] remains are to be searched for their source clipping, […]

Should probably be something like "original instance" or "initiating instance" rather than "clipping".

Under no circumstances is SCP-1297 to be transported by air, nor should it be allowed within 40 km of the nearest population center of 500 persons or more.

"should", not "is"

Five hundred (500).

[description snipped]

I can't help but wonder if this could be shortened, because it doesn't flow very well and it really, really needs to be simplified.

SCP-1297-1 refers to a defensive mechanism employed by SCP-1297 in perpetuating periods of activity, […]

Within five minutes of emergence, SCP-1297-1 will solidify around its source clipping in a radially symmetric pattern similar to an invertebrate of class Asteroidea.

You have inconsistent definitions of -1. One says it's a defense mechanism, the other says it's the ooze that comes out when said defense mechanism triggers.

Not to mention, "defense mechanism" is a misnomer because it's not actually defending itself, it's just forcing itself to activate. "an event that results in the autoactivation of SCP-1297's anomalous property that can occur during extended periods of inactivity" would be more accurate.

Addendum 1:

Normally addendum titles are something like "Addendum 1297-01: Incident Log, ██/██/██"

Early efforts on containment assumed the object to be of Euclid class, and were focused primarily upon preventing the emergence and manifestation of SCP-1297-1.

As Echo often points out, this is absurd. There is little to no connection between Euclid-class objects, and the containment for one Euclid-class object may have absolutely no effect on another. Rather than use one of the S/E/K classifications, you should state the specific false assumptions that caused this incident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, missed that. Now I want to double my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I was confused by the use of the discredited and unscientific term "humor", until I realized that the item's properties are likely affected the documentation itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like your progress here, you've come a long way with this draft. I'm very happy to have helped you with this, and am extremely pleased to be upvoting it. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You did it. you made a keter jar of toenails. I don't know if I am supposed to blame you or djoric. Maybe both. Have your goddamn upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice and strange, just the way I like 'em. Nice twist at the end, too. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me far too long to realise the implications of the package coming from 2049.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, sorry, didn't mean to hit reply there, Scantron.
Ignore that, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had to look up what humor and Asteroidea meant, but it was worth it.
Although, humor seems like too much of a broad term here.
Is it bile or some other kind of bodily fluid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what? This is fucking weird and I like it. The tone of the note was delightful, too.

Grammar issue, though. I see you doing this:

Words words words, however words words words.

When you should be doing this:

Words words words; however, words words words.

Fix that pls.
